coredata.dc:title: Understanding of the potential role of carbon dots in promoting interspecies electron transfer process in anaerobic co-digestion under magnetic field: Focusing on methane and hydrogen production

coredata.dc:description: Anaerobic digestion shows great potential for biomass energy recovery, however, the low electron transfer efficiency between microorganisms limits overall anaerobic digestion performance. In this work, aloe peel-derived carbon dots (carbon quantum dots, CQDs) were designed and investigated as accelerants for anaerobic digestion systems under an applied magnetic field to promote the interspecific electron transfer (IET) efficiency in methane and hydrogen production processes. Compared with the control group (188.9 mL/g VS, 4.2 mL/g VS, 34.7 %, and 26.3 %), the applied magnetic field (5 mT) significantly increased methane yield (256.1 mL/g VS), hydrogen yield (7.0 mL/g VS), total chemical oxygen demand reduction (41.7 %), and energy conversion efficiency (35.7 %). The introduction of the CQDs (0.18–0.45 wt.%) under a magnetic field (5 mT) has further positive effects on digestion performance. The experimental group incorporating 0.36 wt.% CQDs under a 5 mT magnetic field obtained the highest methane yield (348.9 mL/g VS), hydrogen yield (9.9 mL/g VS), and total chemical oxygen demand reduction (58.4 %), and thus the energy recovery efficiency was increased by 85 % compared with control group. The digestate incorporating 0.36 wt.% CQDs exhibited excellent thermal stability (59.71 %) and total nutrient content (38.17 g/kg), and has great potential as a main ingredient in compound fertilizer. The potential role of the magnetic field and CQDs in enhancing hydrogen interspecies electron transfer (HIET) and direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) for methane and hydrogen production is further illustrated, providing a feasible solution for improving energy recovery from biomass.

head.ce:title: Understanding of the potential role of carbon dots in promoting interspecies electron transfer process in anaerobic co-digestion under magnetic field: Focusing on methane and hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Materials Laboratory (FML), School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Functional Materials Laboratory (FML), School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi’an University of Architecture and Technology, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710055, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anaerobic digestion shows great potential for biomass energy recovery, however, the low electron transfer efficiency between microorganisms limits overall anaerobic digestion performance. In this work, aloe peel-derived carbon dots (carbon quantum dots, CQDs) were designed and investigated as accelerants for anaerobic digestion systems under an applied magnetic field to promote the interspecific electron transfer (IET) efficiency in methane and hydrogen production processes. Compared with the control group (188.9 mL/g VS, 4.2 mL/g VS, 34.7 %, and 26.3 %), the applied magnetic field (5 mT) significantly increased methane yield (256.1 mL/g VS), hydrogen yield (7.0 mL/g VS), total chemical oxygen demand reduction (41.7 %), and energy conversion efficiency (35.7 %). The introduction of the CQDs (0.18–0.45 wt.%) under a magnetic field (5 mT) has further positive effects on digestion performance. The experimental group incorporating 0.36 wt.% CQDs under a 5 mT magnetic field obtained the highest methane yield (348.9 mL/g VS), hydrogen yield (9.9 mL/g VS), and total chemical oxygen demand reduction (58.4 %), and thus the energy recovery efficiency was increased by 85 % compared with control group. The digestate incorporating 0.36 wt.% CQDs exhibited excellent thermal stability (59.71 %) and total nutrient content (38.17 g/kg), and has great potential as a main ingredient in compound fertilizer. The potential role of the magnetic field and CQDs in enhancing hydrogen interspecies electron transfer (HIET) and direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) for methane and hydrogen production is further illustrated, providing a feasible solution for improving energy recovery from biomass.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Upgrading organic waste into fertilizer and fuel through anaerobic digestion is an effective way to recycle renewable energy [1–3]. Anaerobic digestion is a common and effective treatment technology for biomass waste, which involves a complex biological conversion process that depends on the syntrophic metabolism of microbes [4,5]. However, these microbial communities are often challenged by poor digestive environments [6] and low interspecific electron transfer (IET) efficiency [7], resulting in poor stability, low organic degradation rates, and low biogas production in anaerobic digestion systems [8–10].

To overcome these difficulties, a magnetic field (MF) has proven to be effective in enhancing anaerobic digestion performance [11–13]. An appropriate MF environment can enhance the activity of the electron transfer system by boosting microbial metabolic activity and the synthesis of microbial extracellular polymeric substances [13,14]. A weak MF (5 mT) in wastewater treatment, significantly increased bacterial activity [15]. Zhao et al. reported that when the optimal MF intensity was 11.4 mT, the total number of microorganisms increased by 85.5 %, the gas yield increased by 19.5 %, and the methane content increased by 20.0 % [16]. Qu et al. showed that MF improves the degradation of lignocellulosic biomass and increases methane production [17]. Previous researches have shown that MF promotes organic degradation and methane production by affecting microbial growth and metabolism. However, the promotion of anaerobic digestion by MF is limited. To become a practical technology, it is necessary to further improve the anaerobic digestion performance by combining various technologies with MF for anaerobic digestion systems.

In contrast, adding accelerants is another effective method to improve anaerobic digestion performance. Carbon materials are commonly used as nonmetallic accelerants in anaerobic digestion systems because of their large specific surface area, good electronic transmission capability, and wide availability [18]. Carbon dots (carbon quantum dots, CQDs), as nanostructured carbon materials, have attracted much attention because of their good biocompatibility, anti-metabolic degradation (not easily degraded), higher specific surface area, and electronic transmission capability compared with ordinary carbon materials [19,20]. Li et al. reported Zn-doped CQDs or N-doped CQDs in anaerobic digestion systems with different substrates, and proved that the CQDs were involved in the DIET process [21]. Liu et al. used the CQDs as accelerants to increase cumulative methane production and total COD (TCOD) removal rate, and the CQDs can enrich the DIET process related with microorganisms [22]. This indicates that CQDs can establish DIET in anaerobic digestion systems with different substrates and improve the anaerobic digestion performance.

It is foreseeable that MF enhances electron production by promoting microbial proliferation and metabolic activity, while CQDs accelerants can be used as a supplement to the effective medium for electron transfer between microorganisms, which will significantly stimulate electron generation and transfer during anaerobic co-digestion (AcoD), thereby optimizing AcoD efficiency. However, there are no reports on the effect of the CQDs accelerants on the hydrogen production efficiency of the AcoD systems under the MF condition. Additionally, the effect of CQDs on the methanogenic pathway of the AcoD system under MF conditions remains unclear. Therefore, this work aimed to explore the effect of CQDs on the AcoD system under a MF. First, the impact of CQDs on the AcoD performance under MF was evaluated by biogas yield, CH4 and H2 yield, energy conversion efficiency, TCOD, total solids (TS) and volatile solids (VS) reduction rates, and thermal stability and fertilizer efficiency of the digestate. Second, the effects of the MF and CQDs on the electron transfer of the AcoD systems were characterized by electrochemical tests. Finally, combined with an analysis of microbial community, the potential understanding for enhancing methanogenic pathways was explored.

According to previous research, cow manure (CM) and aloe peel waste (APW) were mixed as co-substrates in 1:3 mass ratio and added to the reactor [1,23]. The CM was collected from a farm in Xi'an, Shaanxi. The APW was obtained from an aloe beverage production plant in Yangling, Shaanxi. The processing method of APW is described in detail in the previous work [24]. The anaerobic digested sludge (AS) from a wastewater treatment plant in Xi'an was used as an inoculum for the AcoD. The detailed characteristic parameters of substrates and inoculum are shown in Table 1 .

The CQDs derived from aloe peel were used as accelerants for the AcoD systems. The CQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method using aloe peel powder. To obtain aloe peel powder, the aloe peel was dried at 100 °C for 24 h and then crushed by a wall breaker until its particle size was less than 300 μm. Next, 0.4 g of aloe peel powder was mixed with 50 mL of deionized water and stirred for 3 h using a magnetic stirrer. The resulting mixture was heated at 190 °C for 5.5 h in a 100 mL PTFE-lined high-pressure reactor. After cooling down naturally to room temperature, the insoluble carbon products were filtered out using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The supernatant was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off: 1000 kDa, JielePu) for 2 days and then vacuum dried at 75 °C for 48 h to obtain CQDs.

Considering the balance between operational efficiency and time cost, we conducted a 35-day laboratory-scale batch experiment to ensure that the microbial community had sufficient time to adapt to the digestion environment and convert most of the degradable organic matter into biogas. The laboratory-scale batch experiments were conducted using the bottles with a working volume of 400 mL as the reactors. A reactor, a conical flask (for gas collection), and a water collection bottle were connected sequentially with rubber tubing and glass tubing for biogas yield measurements. CM and APW were mixed as co-substrates in 1:3 mass ratio and added to the reactor. The co-substrate and inoculum were added to the reactor at a mass ratio of 3:7 based on optimal TS content (6–10 %) [2,25]. During the digestion, the reactor was in a weak MF (5 mT) and constant temperature water (36 ± 1 °C) [12,26]. The MF environment is provided by two magnets (1 cm × 5 cm × 10 cm) placed on either side of the reactor. The distance between the magnets is controlled to achieve a central MF intensity of 5 mT within the reactor.

Different concentrations (0.18, 0.27, 0.36 and 0.45 wt.%, based on the total mass of substrates and inoculum) of CQDs were added into the AcoD under weak MF conditions (5 mT). The control group (CK) reactor was loaded with only co-substrates (CM and APW) and inoculum (AS) without accelerants (CQDs) and MF. Compared to CK, the CKMF reactor was externalized with a weak MF of 5 mT. Batch experiments were set up with six groups: CKMF, CQDs-0.18MF, CQDs-0.27MF, CQDs-0.36MF, CQDs-0.45MF, and CK. All experimental groups were in triplicate.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of CQDs were collected on an automatic powder XRD (D/Max2200, America). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of the samples were measured by an UV–vis spectrometer (Youke TN600, China). The presence of surface functional groups on CQDs samples was detected by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher IS50, America). Microscopic images of CQDs were taken by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200X, America). Biogas production was measured at 24 h intervals by the drainage collection method. CH4 and H2 contents in biogas were determined using a gas chromatograph (GC9790, China). The elemental content of the substrate was determined by an elemental analyzer (Vario EL III, Germany). TS and VS were determined using standard methods (APHA, 2005). TCOD of digestates was determined by spectrophotometer (UV2102c, China) and COD digester (DBR200, USA). The pH in the AcoD system was tested every five days using an electronic pH meter (PHS-3C, China). Thermal analysis based on thermogravimetry (TG) was performed using a thermal analyzer (STA449F3, Germany). The total nutrient content of digestate was measured by standard method (NY/T 525–2021).

The electron exchange capacity (EEC) of CQDs and the electrochemical properties of the anaerobic digestions were determined at an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, China). The EEC of CQDs is the sum of their electron-accepting capacity (EAC) and electron-donating capacity (EDC). EAC and EDC were measured by mediated electrochemical reduction (MER) and mediated electrochemical oxidation (MEO), respectively. MEO and MER tests [1,11,27] were performed in a three-electrode system at room temperature, where the working, reference and counter electrodes in the three-electrode system were glassy carbon electrode, saturated silver chloride (Ag/AgCl), and graphite rod electrode, respectively. For MEO (vs. SHE, Eh = 0.61 V) and MER (vs. SHE, Eh = −0.49 V), diquat dibromide monohydrate (DQ) and 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) were used as electron shuttling agents. For the test, 2 mL of the electron shuttling agent (DQ or ABTS, 11 mM) was added to 75 mL of a buffer solution, which was a mixture of 0.1 M KCl and 0.1 M K2HPO4 at pH = 7. Then, after passing N2 through the mixed solution for 30 min, 4 g/L of CQDs solution was added. The EAC and EDC can be calculated from the obtained current peaks by using Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), respectively.

where Ired is reduction current after MER baseline correction; Iox is oxidation current after MEO baseline correction; F is the Faraday constant (=96485 s A/mol e-); mCQDs is the mass of the CQDs (g).

After 35 days of digestion, we directly installed a three-electrode system onto the main reactor and then sequentially employed linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) to investigate the electrochemical performance of the AcoD system [1,7]. Unlike the EEC test, graphite rods were used as working electrodes in CV and LSV tests. The scan rate is 50 mV/s with a potential range of −0.9–0.9 V relative to Ag/Ag+ in CV measurement. The scan rate of LSV is 50 mV/s with a potential range of −1.0–0.2 V relative to Ag/Ag+.

After 35 days of digestion, bacterial and archaeal genomic DNA was obtained from the reactors of the CK, CKMF and the CQDs-0.36MF. The microbial communities in the reactors were analyzed by 16S rRNA gene sequencing technology. The amplicon PCR primers used were (CCTACGGGNGGCWGCAG) and (GACTACHVGGGTATCTAATCC). After sequencing using the Illumina MiSeq system, the microorganisms were classified by comparison with the RDP database.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis, energy conversion efficiency, and anaerobic biodegradability
ce:section.ce:para:
As a classical model often used in anaerobic digestion to simulate digestive processes, the modified Gompertz model (Eq. (3)) was used to verify the confidence of the data in the experiment.

The energy conversion efficiency (η) of the AcoD system is calculated by Eq. (4) and Eq. (5). η is defined as the ratio of the heating value (HV) of recovered methane and hydrogen to that of the co-substrate [28].

Where VCH4 and VH2 are the yield of methane and hydrogen; HVCH4 (35.8 kJ/L), HVH2 (12.8 kJ/L), HVCM , HVAPW and HVAS represent the HV of CH4, H2, CM, APW, and AS, respectively [29,30]; mCM , mAPW , and mAS represent the dry weight of CM, APW, and AS, respectively; C, H, O, and N are the content of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen in the dry weight of organic matter (wt.% on TS), respectively.

The degree of biodegradability of the substrate was evaluated by calculating the ratio of cumulative methane production to theoretical methane production (Eq. (6)) within 35 days in the reactor [31,32].

Where VCH4 is the cumulative yield of methane; TV CH4 is the theoretical methane yield, which is determined by the COD content in the reactor. The theoretical value of methane produced per gram of COD is 350 mL [32].

Statistical analysis was performed with the help of IBM SPSS Statistics 27 software. The results of the experiment were shown as mean ± standard deviation. Differences in experimental data were estimated using one-way analysis of variance or single sample T test.

The morphology and composition of CQDs were characterized by XRD patterns, TEM images, FTIR spectrum, and UV–vis absorption spectrum. The crystal structure of the obtained CQDs was investigated by XRD, as shown in Fig. 1
a. The wide peak at 22.0° in XRD patterns indicates that CQDs are mainly amorphous carbon. This can be attributed to the presence of carbonaceous (organic) compounds in aloe peel waste [33]. TEM and HRTEM images (Fig. 1 b) show that the CQDs samples are quasi-spherical with a lateral size of about 5 nm and well dispersed. CQDs can be internalized by cells and bind to cell structures due to their small size. These internalized CQDs not only enhance bacterial conductivity but also stimulate cellular physiological reactions to improve the efficiency of extracellular electron transfer [19]. The chemical composition of CQDs was analyzed by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1 c). The following characteristic peaks were obtained in FTIR analysis: –OH stretching vibration at 3420 cm−1 and 1092 cm−1, C–H stretching vibration at 2931 cm−1, and O=C–O– stretching vibration at 1593 cm−1 and 1421 cm−1, which were consistent with the previous work [34]. As shown in Fig. 1 d, the peak of the UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs near 275 nm is considered to be the π–π* transition of C=C or the n–π* transition of C=O. Generally, the functional groups on the surface will affect the electron transfer characteristics of CQDs [22]. Among the functional groups mentioned above, the C=O functional groups in O=C−O−, and –OH function groups can help the CQDs mediate the electrons [27], the C=C bond can promote the electron transfer [1,22]. Wang et al. demonstrated that biochar, rich in C=C, C=O, and −OH functional groups, exhibits strong capabilities as an electron mediator or shuttle, along with excellent conductivity [35]. This biochar enriches the species within the Methanosarcina and Methanothrix species, thereby enhancing methane production [35]. These functional groups have electron-donating or electron-withdrawing ability, which plays a key role in mediating electron transfer between microbial species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of MF and CQDs accelerants on AcoD performance

ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 2
a shows the daily biogas yields of the AcoD. The biogas production process in all reactors was carried out for 35 days until the daily biogas production remained low and showed a decreasing trend. The CK had two biogas yields peaks (11th and 21st days), and the groups with MF had three biogas yields peaks (Fig. 2 b). According to the literature [24,27], the first peak represents the degradation of carbohydrates, and the other peaks represent large complex organic matter (lignin and protein, etc.) degradation. This indicates that due to MF, the process of converting large complex organic matter into biogas was accelerated, forming a biogas yields peak that is significantly different from the CK [1]. In addition, the addition of the CQDs further hastened the degradation of complex organic matter, leading to an earlier occurrence of the biogas yield peak. Compared with CKMF and CQDs-0.18MF, the first peak, second peak, and third peak of CQDs-0.27MF moved forward by 2, 4, and 5 days, respectively, and the peaks of CQDs-0.36MF were by 3, 6, and 6 days earlier, respectively. These findings indicate that MF and CQDs significantly increased the biogas production rate of AcoD system.

Fig. 2 c shows the measurement results of biogas production and the fitting results of the modified Gompertz model. The correlation coefficients are listed in Table 2 . In each experimental group, the predicted results were in good agreement with the measured results, and the correlation coefficients were greater than 0.99. The fitting results indicate that the use of MF and CQDs can significantly enhance both Pm and Rm. Compared to the CK, the Pm was increased by 15.2–16.6 %, and the Rm was increased by 8.4 %–40.6 %. Similarly, the peak of daily biogas production of the AcoD system with MF and CQDs, as observed in Fig. 2 a, is higher, consistent with the fitting results. Although the actual results show that MF and CQDs promoted an earlier peak time (see Fig. 2 b) and faster recovery from acidification (see Fig. 3
a), the fitting results reveal that MF and CQDs did not significantly affect the reduction of lag time (λ), which may be due to the prolonged acid inhibition. Similar phenomenon also was observed by Sun et al. [6]. The longer lag period of all groups can be attributed to the fact that we did not add buffer (e.g. NaHCO3) in the experiment. Without the addition of these buffers, the system required additional time at the beginning of digestion to establish an optimal pH environment, resulting in a uniform prolongation of the lag period in all samples. Among them, the P(t) of CQDs-0.36MF was the highest (581.2 mL/g VS), Rm was 28.4 mL/g VS/day, and the lag time (λ) was the shortest (4.4 days). It shows that CQDs-0.36MF effectively accelerates the production of biogas and improves the gas production potential.

The use of CQDs and MF in AcoD has a positive effect on CH4 production (Fig. 2 d-e). CH4 and biogas yields were 256.1 and 486.2 mL/g VS for CKMF, 300.0 and 515.4 mL/g VS for CQDs-0.18MF, 327.2 and 534.2 mL/g VS for CQDs-0.27MF, 348.90 and 557.2 mL/g VS for CQDs-0.36MF, 287.5 and 508.5 mL/g VS for CQDs-0.45MF, and 188.9 and 450.2 mL/g VS for CK. Compared to the CK, the AcoD system augmented with MF and CQDs has an increase in CH4 yield (49.2–84.7 %) and biogas yield (12.6–23.8 %), outperforming the systems that employed only CQDs in previous work [22]. The biogas production of the system with the highest CQDs dosage added was slightly lower. The possible reason is that the cytotoxicity produced by higher concentrations of CQDs may inhibit the growth or metabolic activity of microorganisms, thus affecting the promotion of CQDs on the AcoD system [33,36]. The CH4 content of each group remained at a low level (9.6–12.5 %) in the first 5 days, increased after the 5th day, and remained stable after the 10th day. The average CH4 content was 42.0 % for CK, 52.7 % for CKMF, 58.2 % for CQDs-0.18MF, 61.3 % for CQDs-0.27MF, 62.6 % for CQDs-0.36MF, and 56.5 % for CQDs-0.45MF, respectively. The above results showed that MF significantly increased methane and biogas production, and the CQDs and MF further improved the gas production performance of AcoD system. This may be attributed to the stimulation of MF on microbial metabolic activity [15] and the support of CQDs to enhance DIET between microorganisms [21,22]. In addition, based on the theoretical methane yield (calculated by the TCOD content) of 628.13 mL CH4/g VS for each reactor, the anaerobic biodegradability was 40.8 % for CKMF, 47.8 % for CQDs-0.18MF, 52.1 % for CQDs-0.27MF, 55.5 % for CQDs-0.36MF, 45.8 % for CQDs-0.45MF, and 30.1 % for CK. This indicates the enhancement of substrate anaerobic biodegradability by MF and CQDs.

In particular, MF and CQDs also have a positive effect on H2 production (Fig. 2 d-e). H2 yield and average content were 4.2 mL/g VS and 0.93 % for CK, 7.0 mL/g VS and 1.34 % for CKMF, 8.1 mL/g VS and 1.57 % for CQDs-0.18MF, 8.4 mL/g VS and 1.57 % for CQDs-0.18MF, 9.9 mL/g VS and 1.77 % for CQDs-0.36MF, 7.9 mL/g VS and 1.56 % for CQDs-0.45MF. The biogas maintained a low hydrogen content, even though the average hydrogen content increased. Therefore, the enhancement of the hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis pathway may also be another factor in the promotion of methane production by MF and CQDs.

In addition to methane and hydrogen production, energy recovery efficiency is also evaluated here (Fig. 2 f). The energy conversion efficiency of CKMF (35.7 %) was 1.36 times that of CK (26.3 %), while that of groups (40.1–48.7 %) with CQDs and MF was 1.52–1.85 times that of CK. This could be attributed to the MF and CQDs improving the digestion environment and enhancing microbial activity, which in turn increases the efficiency of substrate degradation and the gas (CH4 and H2) production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pH values, TCOD reduction rates, TS and VS reductions
ce:section.ce:para: The change of pH within each group is shown in Fig. 3 a. The initial pH of different groups was between 7.03 and 7.24. In the start-up phase of digestion (the first 5 days), the pH value decreased rapidly due to the production of organic acids. At this stage, easily degradable macromolecules, including carbohydrates, are hydrolyzed into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), resulting in acidification of the digestive environment and a decrease in pH. Similar phenomena have also been observed by Guo et al. and Yang et al. [37,38]. After 5th day, the pH levels in each group began to rise gradually due to the consumption of VFAs by methanogens [1]. The pH variation during digestion for each group ranged from 5.68 to 7.50, which falls within the controllable range of 5.5–8.2 [39,40]. The maximum biogas production on the 10th day described in Fig. 2 a may be closely related to the accumulation and transformation of VFAs and the acid buffering capacity of the AcoD system. Initially, on the fifth day, the pH value reached its lowest point, indicating the highest level of VFAs accumulation. These VFAs were then converted into biogas, leading to a peak in gas production around day 10. Furthermore, our microbial community analysis (see Fig. 6 c) demonstrated an enrichment of acid-resistant methanogens, such as Methanosarcina, Methanobacterium, Methanomassiliicoccus, and Methanobrevibacter, in the anaerobic digestion system. Previous literature suggests that many species within these genera are capable of growing and metabolizing under low pH conditions (pH < 6.0) [41], which explains the continued increase in biogas production even at lower pH levels. Additionally, methane content testing during days 5–10 revealed that methane levels remained low, indicating that the biogas production peak was accompanied by lots of CO2. The slow rate of pH increased from day 5 (5.69) to day 10 (5.75) in the CK was mainly related to excessive acidification due to substrate degradation. Compared with CK, the pH recovery rate of the groups with MF or CQDs was faster, which represented a faster rate of organic acid consumption, consistent with increased gas production and methane content. This indicates that the system with MF has better buffering performance and greater stability, and recovers from stagnation earlier due to the effect of CQDs.

TCOD can represent the total amount of biomass organic matter in the digestate. The TCOD reduction rate of the CK, CKMF, CQDs-0.18MF, CQDs-0.27MF, CQDs-0.36MF, and CQDs-0.45MF was 34.7 %, 41.7 %, 51.1 %, 54.1 %, 58.4 %, and 48.2 % (Fig. 3 b). The reduction rate of TCOD in CKMF group was higher than that in CK group. With the increase of CQDs addition, TCOD degradation rate increased first and then decreased. These results are consistent with the biogas yield and CH4 yield, which proves that MF and CQDs promote the transformation of organic matter in the substrate into biogas. TS and VS reduction rates are shown in Fig. 3 c-d. Similar to the results for TCOD removal rates, MF and CQDs contributed to a greater reduction in TS and VS of the AcoD. The degradation rates of TS and VS in the CQDs-0.36MF (49.8 % and 47.9 %) were much higher than CK (36.5 % and 38.1 %) and CKMF (41.2 % and 42.9 %). This indicates that the biodegradation of substrates by the AcoD was significantly enhanced by CQDs and MF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs accelerants and MF on digestate

ce:section.ce:para:
The stability of digestate is an important indicator used to assess the magnitude of its potential as a biofertilizer. Direct discharge of unstable digestate is prone to produce harmful gases and pathogens that can pose a threat to the quality of the receiving environment [1,2] and therefore will not be used as a component of biofertilizer. Thermal analysis based on TG, differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), and differential thermogravimetry analysis (DTG) is considered a general technique to investigate the thermal stability of digestate. The percentage of residual mass on the TG curve is usually used as an indicator of thermal stability. The TG curve (Fig. 4
a) shows the change in the percentage of mass of the digestate in each reactor during the pyrolysis process, which is roughly divided into three stages [42,43]. First, the small amount of mass loss (5.27–6.02 %) in the first stage (below 180 °C) is due to the removal of digestion product water and small molecular organic matter. Then, the main mass loss (31.38–36.12 %) in the second stage (180–550 °C) is due to the decomposition of easily degradable organic matter (proteins, hemicellulose, and cellulose, etc.). Finally, in the third stage (above 550 °C), inorganic minerals and inert macromolecular complex organic matter (lignin, aromatic compounds, etc.) are decomposed and a weak loss of mass (3.30–3.76 %) is observed.

It is generally considered that the less mass loss of digestate after pyrolysis, the better its stability [7]. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the total mass loss of digestate in TG analysis for CK, CKMF, CQDs-0.18MF, CQDs-0.27MF, and CQDs-0.36MF and CQDs-0.45MF groups were 45.45 %, 45.09 %, 40.63 %, 40.62 %, 40.29 %, and 41.32 % respectively. This indicates that the AcoD system with CQDs under MF has a more complete digestion process, and the substrate utilization rate by microorganisms is higher. Compared to CK (6.02 % and 36.12 %) and CKMF (5.75 % and 35.80 %), the digestate of the groups with CQDs under MF lost less mass at first stage and at second stage (5.27–5.65 % and 31.38–32.03 %). These results demonstrated that there are fewer unstable organic compounds in the digestate from the group with CQDs under MF, and the utilization of easily degradable organic matter by microorganisms is enhanced.

The two peaks near 300 °C and 500 °C on the DTG (Fig. 4 c) and DSC (Fig. 4 d) curves correspond to the second stage on the TG curve. The peak on the DTG curve (Fig. 4 c) located at about 650 °C corresponds to the third stage of the TG curve. Due to the complete degradation of unstable organic matter during anaerobic digestion, the digestate was relatively enriched in inorganic minerals and/or macromolecular organic matter that is difficult to biodegrade [43]. The CKMF group (3.54 %) and the group with CQDs and MF (3.58–3.76 %) had more mass loss in the third stage than the CK group (3.30 %) (p < 0.05), indicating that the digestates of the group with CQDs and MF was more stable and contained less easily degradable organic matter. Additionally, the removal rates of TCOD, TS, and VS (as shown in Fig. 3) further indicate that the digestate from the group with CQDs under MF contains fewer readily degradable organic substances (p < 0.05), which also demonstrates the improved stability of the digestate. Totally, when MF or CQD were applied, several observations were made: the low mass loss of stage I means that the utilization of the hydrocarbons with small molecular weight by microorganisms has increased; the low mass loss of stage II suggested an increased utilization of simple organic matter (such as proteins, hemicellulose, and cellulose) by microorganisms; the high mass loss of stage III implied an increased utilization of degradable organic matter by microorganisms; and thus the higher TN and TP contents (see Table 3 ) than the existing digestion sludge mean that the growth rate of microorganisms is high. The results of the above thermal analysis suggest that MF and CQDs promoted the degradation of biomass and promoted the retention of more stable digestion products in the AcoD system.

The digestate, abundant in nutrients (organic matter, macronutrients, and trace elements), can be used as a soil nutrient to promote crop growth for sustainable development [23,24]. As shown in Table 3, according to the technical index of organic fertilizer NY525-2021 of China, the mass fraction of total nutrients of organic fertilizer must reach 4 % [44]. The CQDs-0.36MF digestate contains total phosphorus (TP), total potassium (TK), total nitrogen (TN), and total nutrients at concentrations of 17.36 g/kg, 9.59 g/kg, 11.22 g/kg, and 38.17 g/kg respectively. The total nutrient content in the CQDs-0.36MF digestate is 38.17 g/kg, which is close to the technical specifications for organic fertilizers under China’s NY525-2021 standard (40 g/kg). The digestate from CQDs-0.36MF has a high total nutrient content, which is likely due to high microbial growth rate promoting the conversion of carbon-rich solid organic matter into methane and carbon dioxide, effectively reducing the carbon content and thereby increasing TN and TP contents in the digestate. The TP, TK, and total nutrient content of digestate in CQDs-0.36MF were much higher than soil (0.06–0.10, 0.10–0.15, and 3.10–4.50 g/kg), but lower than bio-organic fertilizer (25.40, 18.00, and 60.50 g/kg); however, the TN content of the digestate (17.36 g/kg) exceeded that of the bio-organic fertilizer (17.10 g/kg), making it suitable for nitrogen-deficient soil or as the main raw material for the production of high nutrient bio-organic fertilizer [1,27]. Note that before the digestate can be utilized as fertilizer, its safety must be ensured. Therefore, the subsequent treatments, such as the removal of pathogens and degradation of toxic substances, are necessary.

According to the economic analysis methods from previous literature [45,46], a comparison of the cost of producing catalysts against the revenue from increased biogas production revealed that CQDs as accelerants are not cost-effective. One of the important reasons is that although CQDs are derived from discarded aloe peels with negligible raw material costs and a non-complex preparation process, there are still a lot of energy costs in its production process due to the low yield of CQDs. Therefore, future research should explore new synthetic methods to increase CQDs yield and improve cost efficiency. However, this analysis overlooks the overall benefits to the entire system. For instance, the addition of CQDs reduces the COD and solid content, significantly lowering the storage and transportation costs of digestate in industrial-scale biogas plants. The digestate can be used to produce compound fertilizers, offering an alternative to reduce the cost of accelerants. In large biogas-scale plants, utilizing biogas for combined heat and power generation can yield additional economic and environmental benefits. Government policies addressing energy and environmental issues, including investment subsidies, can reduce the costs for large-scale biogas plants. Notably, the by-product of producing CQDs is almost biochar, which, according to the previous research [47,48], can also serve as an accelerant to enhance the biogas production and COD degradation efficiency of anaerobic digestion. Co-existence of CQDs and biochar as accelerants can further improve energy utilization rates, helping to offset the costs of producing CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs accelerants and MF on electrochemical performance of AcoD systems
ce:section.ce:para:
The accelerants of the CQDs were characterized for their EAC and EDC. Fig. 5
a-b illustrates a positive correlation between the number of exchange electrons (Q) in MER and MEO tests and the mass of CQDs, with correlation coefficients of 0.99 and 0.96, respectively, similar to those reported previously. EEC (1.17 μmol e-/g CQDs) was the sum of EDC and EAC, where EDC (0.82 μmol e-/g CQDs) was much larger than EAC (0.35 μmol e-/g CQDs), possibly due to the influence of surface functional groups on the CQDs [27]. The above results indicate that CQDs in the anaerobic digestion system have the ability to accept electrons from other electron donors (such as electrogenic bacteria, intermediate metabolites) and provide electrons to other electron acceptors (such as methanogens).

The conductivity of the anaerobic digestion system can be determined by LSV testing of the digestate, and the higher the limiting current value of the LSV curve, the higher the electron transfer capacity [7]. As shown in Fig. 5 c, the current of CQDs-0.36MF (0.2880 mA, absolute value) was higher than CKMF (0.2417 mA, absolute value) and CK (0.2211 mA, absolute value) groups at the same potential (−0.80 V, diffusion zone), indicating that the introduction of CQDs along with the use of the MF improved the electron transfer within the AcoD system. Similarly, Wang et al. enhanced the current of the anaerobic digestion system from 4.61 × 10−4 to 6.47 × 10−4 A at the same potential (0.80 V, diffusion region) by adding the bag-filter gas dust [7].

It has been reported that extracellular electron acceptors can accept electrons transferred by electrogenic bacteria and are widely distributed in the anaerobic digestion system [49]. The larger the peak current of the redox peak of the CV curve, the stronger the redox characteristics, and the more redox active components in the system that can be used as electron shuttles to accelerate indirect extracellular electron transfer [49], resulting in better conductivity [7]. As shown in Fig. 5 d, the CV curves of CQDs-0.36MF group and CKMF group showed obvious oxidation peaks at 750 mV, and the oxidation currents were 5.477 mA and 1.048 mA, respectively. CK group had no obvious oxidation peak. The CV curve of CQDs-0.36MF group showed an obvious reduction peak at −675 mV, and the reduction current intensity was −4.642 mA. Similarly, the redox peak is more obvious in the anaerobic digestion system with Fe-loaded activated carbon than in the control group without additive [49]. In addition, the relatively large CV curve integral area indicates that CQDs-0.36MF group has a higher specific capacitance. CQDs and MF may promote microbial metabolism of more redox mediators and CQDs themselves give AcoD higher electronic storage capacity [50]. These results suggest that the enrichment of redox substances is much lower in the absence of external driving forces such as CQDs or MF. Therefore, from the LSV and CV curves, CQDs and MF significantly promoted the electron transfer and redox ability within the system, potentially mediating the DIET in the process of methanogenesis, thereby promoting organic matter degradation and CH4 production.

Fig. 6 shows the microbial community structure in CK, CKMF, and CQDs-0.36MF. At the genus level, the bacterial community (Fig. 6 a) of CK was mainly composed of Proteiniclasticum (39.99 %), Syntrophomonas (7.18 %), and Petrimonas (5.35 %). Under the action of an external MF, the dominant bacteria of CKMF and CQDs-0.36MF became Acinetobacter (49.00 % and 44.90 %), Clostridium (7.42 % and 13.71 %), and Petrimonas (12.71 % and 7.12 %). The bacteria of these three genera are all electrogenic bacteria [38,51,52], which can carry out extracellular respiration and have electrochemical activity. Specifically, electroactive bacteria can transfer electrons produced inside cells to extracellular electron acceptors through extracellular respiration [53,54]. This indicated that MF and CQDs improved the composition of microbial community, increased the enrichment of electrogenic bacteria, and which will be useful to enhance the DIET in AcoD. As shown in Fig. 6 b, the abundances of Acinetobacter (49.00 %), Clostridium (7.42 %), and Petrimonas (12.71 %) in CKMF were exceeded that in CK, suggesting a selective enrichment effect of MF on these specific electroactive bacteria and hydrogen-producing bacteria. Notably, the relative abundance of Clostridium in CQDs-0.36MF (13.71 %) was significantly higher than that in CKMF (7.42 %). Clostridium has type IV pili, it has been reported that Clostridium participates in the hydrogen production from acetic acid by syntrophic oxidation with hydrogenotrophic methanogens and participates in the DIET process as an electron donor microorganism [54,55]. In addition, according to work of Chen et al. [56], MF enhanced the H2-related metabolic pathway of species in Clostridium, and the H2 production and production rate reached the maximum when 3.2 mT MF was applied. Therefore, the enrichment in hydrogen-producing bacteria by MF and CQDs and the enhanced metabolic activity of hydrogen-producing bacteria by MF can explain the increase in hydrogen production at Section 3.2.1 .

As shown in Fig. 6 c, the archaeal community analysis at the genus level showed that the methanogens with the highest relative abundance in CK, CKMF, CQDs-0.36MF were all Methanosarcina. Methanosarcina can produce methane through three major pathways (hydrogenotrophic, acetoclastic, and methylotrophic) and is an electrotrophic methanogen widely reported to participate in DIET with c-type cytochromes [53]. Methanobacterium was another proven electrotrophic methanogen with DIET ability [52,53], and its relative abundance in CKMF (33.34 %) and CQDs-0.36MF (31.10 %) was significantly higher than that in CK (4.38 %). Therefore, methanogens that can participate in DIET were significantly enriched under the action of MF and CQDs. The relative abundances of Methanomassiliicoccus and Methanobrevibacter were also significantly increased in CKMF (8.23 % and 10.24 %) and CQDs-0.36MF (23.17 % and 5.62 %) compared with CK (5.47 % and 2.23 %). Interestingly, all the four methanogens can use hydrogen to carry electrons for methane production through the hydrogen interspecies electron transfer (HIET) pathway [53,57]. This indicates that MF and CQDs not only enriched electrotrophic methanogens but also promoted the growth of hydrogenotrophic methanogens. Fig. 6 d shows that the total relative abundances of electrotrophic methanogens (Methanosarcina and Methanobacterium) and hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Methanosarcina, Methanobacterium, Methanomassiliicoccus, and Methanobrevibacter) in CKMF (67.17 % and 85.64 %) was higher than that in CK (56.01 % and 63.71 %), indicating that MF promoted the growth of these electrotrophic methanogens and hydrogenotrophic methanogens.

Overall, the addition of CQDs to the AcoD system under magnetic field enriched specific microorganisms that can participate in DIET and HIET, confirming the potential role of MF and CQDs in promoting DIET and HIET. These results verified the impact of MF and CQDs on the performance of H2 and CH4 production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Understanding of enhanced methanogenesis in AcoD with carbon dots under magnetic field
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the enhanced methanogenesis in AcoD systems with carbon dots under a magnetic field, for example, the CQDs-0.36MF group obtained the highest production of H2 and CH4 and the highest degradation rate of organic matter compared with other groups, we elaborated a schematic illustration, as illustrated in Fig. 7 , to further clarify the potential role of MF and CQDs in promoting HIET and DIET.

Anaerobic digestion involves three sequential stages (Fig. 7 a): hydrolysis, hydrogen/acetic acid production, and methanogenesis. In the hydrolysis stage, complex organics such as cellulose and proteins are decomposed into simple organics (volatile fatty acids, VFAs) by hydrolytic bacteria and their enzymes [22,47]. During the hydrogen/acetic acid production stage [1,24], the fermentation bacteria degrade simple organic matter to produce acetate (CH3COOH), methyl compound (CH3X), carbon dioxide (CO2), hydrogen (H2) and other small molecular compounds. During methanogenesis, methanogens use these small molecule compounds to produce CH4. CH4 is produced through different metabolic pathways. Specifically, methylotrophic and acetotrophic methanogens convert methyl compounds and acetates into CH4, respectively, which can be expressed using the two chemical formulations (① CH3X → CH4; ② CH3COOH → CO2 + CH4) [1,48]. In addition, there are two other important processes. One is the hydrogen interspecies electron transfer (HIET) process, in which the hydrogenotrophic methanogens use hydrogen to reduce CO2 and produce CH4, as expressed in the equation (③ 4H2 + CO2 → CH4 + 2H2O, HIET process) [2]. The other is the direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET) process, in which the fermentation bacteria can also directly transfer electrons through conductive pili and cytochrome to methanogens, which can reduce CO2 to CH4 (④ 8e-+8H++CO2 → CH4 + 2H2O, DIET process) [24,58]. In the HIET process, H2 acts as an electron carrier, whereas in the DIET process, electrons are transferred or mediated directly from the fermentation bacteria to the methanogens.

The DIET is facilitated by a conductive biofilm matrix, which includes electrically conductive pili (e-pili), C-type cytochromes, and extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) [59–61]. E-pili, protein nanowires rooted in the cell membrane, enable long-range electron transfer to distant electron acceptors or neighboring cells [59]. C-type cytochromes, redox proteins embedded in the membrane, serve as intracellular electron mediators [60]. EPS, comprising natural polymers that envelop the cell, contribute to electron conduction [61]. Collectively, these components form a bioelectrical network within the biofilm, promoting electron flow between cells and their surroundings.

When the CQDs were added to the AcoD system, their potential role can be illustrated from two aspects, as illustrated in Fig. 7 b. On the one hand, the CQDs, with a small size of approximately 5 nm (confirmed by TEM in Fig. 1 b) and good biocompatibility, dispersed in the AcoD system (specifically, in the intracellular, EPS, and digestate) can construct a conductive network, as illustrated in the blue dot-short line cycle (1) in Fig. 7 b. More specifically, CQDs can be embedded in the cell membrane, the CQDs can bind cell structures (i.e., e-pili, and c-type cytochromes) through non-covalent bonds, such as electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds, which can act as electronic conduits [22,62]. As a result, the CQDs embedded in the cell membrane, the CQDs adsorbed on pili, and the CQDs adsorbed on cytochrome, together, can construct a conductive network.

However, an electron transport channel was fabricated in the conductive network, as illustrated in the red dotted short arrow line (2) in Fig. 7 b. The CQDs stimulated the fermentation bacteria to generate electrons. The generated electrons were transferred from the intracellular to the extracellular space (i), the generated electrons were transferred between the fermentative bacteria and methanogens (ii), the generated electrons were transferred from the extracellular to the intracellular space (iii), and finally, the electrons were accepted by the methanogens. In the electron transport process, the fermentation bacteria, as electron donors, provide or supply electrons, whereas the methanogen, as the electron acceptor, accepts electrons. The constructed conductive network enhances the electron transfer in the electron transport channel, and thus enhances the interspecies electron transfer (including HIET and DIET) processes in the AcoD systems.

Experimental evidence confirmed and supported the enhanced HIET and DIET processes in the AcoD systems. For example, the CQDs have excellent EEC (1.17 μmol e-/g, see Fig. 5 a-b) that promote the IET in the electron transport channel. The larger limiting current in the LSV (0.2880 mA, see Fig. 5 c) testified that CQDs could improve the electron transfer capability of the AcoD system. The higher redox peaks in the CV (−4.642 mA and 5.477 mA, see Fig. 5 d) confirmed that the CQDs increased redox activity and microbial metabolic activity of the AcoD system. Moreover, adding the CQDs into the AcoD systems can enrich the microorganisms that participate in the HIET and DIET processes (as discussed in Section 3.5 ). The enhanced HIET and DIET processes in the AcoD systems can be understood from the MF perspective. First, when MF was applied, the abundances of fermentative bacteria (more specifically, Acinetobacter, Clostridium, and Petrimonas) and methanogens (more specifically, Methanobacterium, Methanomassiliicoccus, and Methanobrevibacter) increased, as illustrated in Fig. 7 b, and confirmed in Fig. 7 c. The enriched fermentative bacteria and methanogens indicate enhanced methanogenesis, thus increasing methane and hydrogen production. Second, compared with the absence of a MF, the relative abundances of hydrogen-producing bacteria (Clostridium) and hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Methanobacterium, Methanomassiliicoccus, and Methanobrevibacter) increased under the action of a MF, as shown in I and II in Fig. 7 c, which promoted the HIET process. Third, under the MF condition, the electroactive bacteria (Acinetobacter, Clostridium, and Petrimonas) and electrotrophic methanogens (Methanobacterium) were enriched, as shown in III and IV of Fig. 7 c, indicating an improvement in the DIET process. In general, an external MF promotes HIET and DIET processes, which the changes in syntrophic microorganisms can confirm. However, under the MF condition, the addition of CQDs promoted an increase in the relative abundances of hydrogen-producing bacteria (Clostridium) and hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Methanosarcina and Methanomassiliicoccus), as shown in I and II in Fig. 7 c, indicating a positive effect of the CQDs on promoting the HIET process. At the same time, after the addition of CQDs, electroactive bacteria (Clostridium) and electrotrophic methanogens (Methanosarcina) were further enriched, as shown in III and IV in Fig. 7 c, indicating that the key role of the CQDs in promoting DIET process. In summary, the addition of CQDs to the AcoD system under the MF condition promoted an increase in the abundances of Clostridium, Methanosarcina, and Methanomassiliicoccus, further confirming the synergistic effect of CQDs and MF, in promoting HIET and DIET.
In general, the contributions of MF and CQDs can be summarized as follows:

1) MF promoted the enrichment of hydrogen-producing bacteria (Clostridium), hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Methanobacterium, Methanomassiliicoccus, and Methanobrevibacter), electroactive bacteria (Acinetobacter, Clostridium, and Petrimonas), and electrotrophic methanogens (Methanobacterium) and enhanced the HIET and DIET processes.

2) CQDs further promoted the enrichment of hydrogen-producing and electroactive bacteria (Clostridium), hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Methanomassiliicoccus), and hydrogenotrophic and electrotrophic methanogens (Methanosarcina) and enhanced the HIET and DIET processes.
3) CQDs construct the electron transport channels and the conductive network, enhancing the efficiency of interspecies electron transfer (including HIET and DIET).
4) The synergistic effect of MF and CQDs promoted the HIET and DIET processes.

Carbon dots can significantly improve the energy recovery of the AcoD systems under applied magnetic field. The CQDs (0.36 wt.%) induced the optimal performance parameters, i.e., CH4 yield (348.9 mL/g VS), H2 yield (9.9 mL/g VS), TCOD reduction rate (58.4 %), energy recovery efficiency (48.7 %), and thermal stability (59.71 %), higher than the control group (188.9 mL/g VS, 4.2 mL/g VS, 34.7 %, 26.3 %, and 54.55 %) and control group with the magnetic field (256.1 mL/g VS, 7.0 mL/g VS, 41.7 %, 35.7 %, and 54.91 %). The magnetic field increased the abundances of the hydrogen-producing bacteria (Clostridium), hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Methanobacterium, Methanomassiliicoccus, and Methanobrevibacter), electroactive bacteria (Acinetobacter, Clostridium, and Petrimonas), and electrotrophic methanogens (Methanobacterium), thereby enhancing HIET and DIET processes. The CQDs were enriched with hydrogen-producing and electroactive bacteria (Clostridium), hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Methanomassiliicoccus), and hydrogenotrophic and electrotrophic methanogens (Methanosarcina). The CQDs further promoted the HIET and DIET processes by constructing electron transport channels and conductive networks. This work provides a feasible strategy for optimizing energy recovery by combining carbon dots with an applied magnetic field to improve the IET efficiency in AcoD systems.

Lijianan Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yasir Abbas: Methodology, Formal analysis. Muhammad Saqlain Jamil: Methodology, Formal analysis. Teng Ke: Methodology. Jiayu Liu: Methodology. Xiaoxue Zhang: Methodology.
